title: Static electricity — why you get zapped touching a door handle
examTip: Static electricity questions almost always ask about electron transfer — never say protons move. State which object gains or loses electrons and what charge each ends up with.
introTips: Think of a time you got a static shock — that tiny spark is the same physics as a lightning bolt, just on a very different scale.
Before reading, try to explain in one sentence why rubbing a balloon on your hair makes it stick to a wall.
Focus on direction of electron transfer — which object gains, which loses, and what charge results.
intro: That small shock you get touching a door handle after walking across a carpet is caused by the same fundamental physics as a lightning strike. In both cases, charge builds up on an insulating surface until it discharges suddenly. Understanding static electricity explains not just natural phenomena like lightning but also how inkjet printers deposit precise ink droplets, how industrial paint sprayers coat car bodies evenly, and why fuel tanker lorries must be earthed before filling up — because a static spark near petrol vapour can be catastrophic.

keyPoints.heading: Charging by friction
keyPoints.content: When two insulating materials are rubbed together, electrons are transferred from one surface to the other. The material that gains electrons becomes negatively charged; the material that loses electrons becomes positively charged. Key rule: only electrons transfer — protons are fixed inside atomic nuclei and cannot be moved by friction. Charge is conserved: the amount of negative charge gained equals the positive charge lost. Examples: a polythene rod rubbed with a cloth gains electrons from the cloth → polythene becomes negative, cloth becomes positive. An acetate rod rubbed with cloth loses electrons to the cloth → acetate becomes positive, cloth becomes negative. Static electricity only builds up on insulators — conductors allow charge to flow away. This is why a metal door handle discharges you instantly (the charge flows through the conductor) but a polythene rod stays charged.
writeBullets: Friction transfers electrons — never protons (protons are fixed in the nucleus)
Object gaining electrons → negatively charged
Object losing electrons → positively charged
Charge is conserved: charge gained = charge lost (equal and opposite)
Polythene + cloth: polythene gains electrons → negative; cloth → positive
Acetate + cloth: acetate loses electrons → positive; cloth → negative

keyTerms.def: The transfer of electrons between two insulating surfaces when rubbed together, causing one to become positive and the other negative.
The total charge before and after friction is the same — charge gained by one object equals charge lost by the other.
A material that does not allow charge to flow through it easily; static charge can build up on insulators.

keyPoints.memTip: Electrons are the movers — think of them as passengers getting off one bus and onto another. The bus that loses passengers (electrons) goes positive; the one that gains goes negative.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The single most common error in static electricity questions is students writing 'protons are transferred'. It must be electrons. Repeat this rule until it is automatic.

keyPoints.heading: Like and unlike charges
keyPoints.content: Unlike (opposite) charges attract each other. Like (same) charges repel each other. This can be demonstrated simply: hang two negatively charged polythene rods near each other on threads — they swing apart (repel). Bring a negatively charged rod near a positively charged acetate rod — they swing toward each other (attract). The force between charges decreases as the distance between them increases. Everyday examples of static attraction and repulsion: clothes clinging from a tumble dryer (different fabrics pick up opposite charges and attract), hair standing on end after pulling off a jumper (hair strands all gain the same charge and repel each other), dust sticking to screens (screen becomes charged and attracts neutral dust particles). Lightning: charge builds up in storm clouds — ice crystals and water droplets collide and transfer charge, making the base of the cloud negatively charged. When enough charge accumulates, a massive spark discharge occurs between cloud and ground — a lightning bolt.
writeBullets: Unlike charges attract; like charges repel
Force between charges decreases with increasing distance
Two charged rods same sign: repel (swing apart on threads)
Two charged rods opposite sign: attract (swing together)
Lightning: charge builds in clouds due to friction between ice crystals and water droplets; discharges to ground
Hair standing on end: strands all same charge → mutual repulsion

keyTerms.def: The force pulling opposite charges together; unlike charges attract.
The force pushing like charges apart; same charges repel.
A massive electrostatic discharge caused by charge build-up in storm clouds; occurs when potential difference becomes large enough to ionise the air.

keyPoints.memTip: Like charges: same = same = sameone gets annoyed = they push each other away (repel). Opposite charges: different attracts different — they want to cancel each other out.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'positive attracts positive' — reinforce repeatedly that LIKE repels and UNLIKE attracts. Getting this backwards costs marks on every static question.

keyPoints.heading: Electric fields
keyPoints.content: A charged object creates an electric field in the space around it. Any other charged object placed in this field experiences a force — objects do not need to touch. An electric field is represented by field lines, which show the direction of force on a small positive test charge. Around an isolated positive charge: field lines point outward (a positive charge would be pushed away). Around an isolated negative charge: field lines point inward (a positive test charge would be pulled in). Between two parallel plates with opposite charges: the field is uniform — field lines are parallel, equally spaced and perpendicular to the plates. A stronger electric field is shown by closer (more dense) field lines. The strength of the electric field decreases with distance from a charged object (similar to gravitational fields). Electrostatic forces act at a distance — this is why a charged rod held near small pieces of paper can attract them without touching.
writeBullets: Charged objects create an electric field in surrounding space — no contact needed
Field lines show direction of force on a positive test charge
Positive charge: field lines radiate outward
Negative charge: field lines radiate inward
Uniform field between parallel plates: parallel, equally spaced lines perpendicular to plates
Closer field lines → stronger electric field

keyTerms.def: A region of space around a charged object where another charge experiences a force.
Lines drawn to represent an electric field; show the direction of force on a positive test charge. Closer lines = stronger field.
A field of constant strength and direction, represented by parallel, equally spaced field lines. Found between two parallel plates with opposite charges.

keyPoints.memTip: Field lines are like arrows showing where a positive charge would go. Positive source: arrows flee outward. Negative source: arrows rush inward (toward negative — opposite attracts).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often draw field lines between parallel plates going from negative to positive plate — remind them field lines go FROM positive TO negative (direction a positive charge moves).

keyPoints.heading: Dangers and uses of static electricity
keyPoints.content: Dangers: Static sparks carry enough energy to ignite flammable vapours. Fuel tanker lorries are connected to the ground by an earthing wire before the fuel hose is connected — charge built up during the journey can discharge safely rather than sparking near fuel vapour. Aircraft build up static charge during flight (friction with air) — bonding cables connected between aircraft and fuelling vehicle drain the charge before refuelling begins. Even a small spark in a fuel vapour environment can cause an explosion. Uses of static electricity: Inkjet printers — droplets of ink are given a charge; the charged droplets are deflected by electric field plates to hit precise positions on the paper, building up text and images. Photocopiers (laser printers) — a drum is charged and laser light removes charge from areas that should stay white; toner powder (charged) sticks only to the charged areas; then fused onto paper by heat. Electrostatic precipitators — used in factory chimneys; charged plates attract soot and dust particles from smoke, removing up to 99% of particles before gases reach the atmosphere. Electrostatic paint spraying — paint droplets are given the same charge (they repel each other, so they spread out evenly); the car body is earthed (opposite charge), attracting all droplets — better coverage, less waste, even coat on complex shapes.
writeBullets: Static spark danger: can ignite flammable vapours — fuel lorries earthed before refuelling
Aircraft bonding cables: drain static charge before fuelling begins
Inkjet printer: charged ink droplets deflected by electric field onto paper
Photocopier: charged drum attracts toner to correct areas; heat fuses toner to paper
Electrostatic precipitator: charged plates attract soot/dust from chimney smoke
Paint spraying: charged droplets repel each other (even spread); earthed car body attracts them

keyTerms.def: Connecting a charged object to the ground with a conductor so charge drains away safely, preventing sparks.
A device in factory chimneys that uses charged plates to attract and remove smoke particles from gases before they are released into the atmosphere.
Uses electrostatically charged ink droplets deflected by electric field plates to build up images on paper.
A conducting wire connecting an aircraft to refuelling equipment to safely discharge static build-up before fuelling.

keyPoints.memTip: For paint spraying: same charge on droplets = they spread out. Opposite charge on car = they're attracted. Both effects together give a perfect even coat with no waste.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions on electrostatic uses often ask students to explain in terms of charge — not just describe what happens. Push students to say 'the charged droplets are repelled by each other and attracted to the earthed surface'.

tables.title: Static electricity — dangers and uses
headers: How it is controlled or exploited
rows: Danger — spark could ignite fuel vapour
Charge builds up as fuel flows through pipes and from the journey
Earthing wire drains charge safely to ground before hose is connected
Friction with air during flight charges the aircraft
Bonding cable connects aircraft to fuelling vehicle before hose is attached
Ink droplets are given a charge by the printer
Electric field plates deflect charged droplets to exact positions on paper
Drum is charged by corona wire; laser removes charge selectively
Charged toner clings to charged areas; heat fuses it to paper
Smoke particles gain charge from high-voltage wire in chimney
Oppositely charged plates attract particles; they fall into collection hoppers
Paint droplets charged by the spray gun
Earthed car body attracts charged droplets; same-charge droplets repel each other for even spread

tables.title: Charging by friction — summary
rows: Electrons transfer FROM cloth TO polythene
Electrons transfer FROM acetate TO cloth
Electrons transfer from hair to balloon
Positive (why hair stands up — strands repel)

0.question: A polythene rod is rubbed with a dry cloth. What has happened to make the rod negatively charged?
options: Electrons have transferred from the cloth to the polythene rod
Protons have transferred from the polythene to the cloth
Electrons have transferred from the polythene to the cloth
The cloth has given the rod extra neutrons
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Electrons move from the cloth onto the polythene — the rod gains electrons and becomes negative. The cloth loses electrons and becomes positive.
0.wrongFeedback: Only electrons transfer — protons and neutrons cannot be moved by friction. The polythene becomes negative because it GAINS electrons from the cloth, not because it loses anything.

1.question: Two polythene rods are both charged negatively and suspended near each other. What will happen?
options: They will repel each other (move apart)
They will attract each other (move together)
One will become positive and attract the other
1.correctFeedback: Correct. Like charges repel — both rods are negative, so they push each other away.
1.wrongFeedback: Remember: like charges REPEL. Both rods are negative (same charge), so they push each other away. Attraction only happens between opposite charges.

2.question: Why must fuel tanker lorries be earthed before connecting the fuel hose?
options: Static charge built up during the journey could discharge as a spark, igniting fuel vapour — earthing drains the charge safely
Earthing increases the pressure in the fuel hose
Earthing makes the lorry a better conductor of electricity
It is a legal requirement with no scientific basis
2.correctFeedback: Spot on. Charge builds up on the tanker during the journey. Earthing gives that charge a safe path to drain away, preventing a spark that could ignite petrol vapour.
2.wrongFeedback: The reason is entirely about static safety. Charge accumulated during the journey could discharge as a spark when the metal hose is connected — near fuel vapour, even a small spark can cause an explosion. Earthing drains the charge to ground first.

3.question: In electrostatic paint spraying, why are the paint droplets given the same charge as each other?
options: So they repel each other and spread out evenly, giving a more uniform coat
So they are attracted to each other and clump together on the surface
To make them fall faster due to gravity
To neutralise the charge on the car body
3.correctFeedback: Perfect. Like charges repel — so droplets with the same charge spread apart from each other rather than clumping. Combined with the earthed car body attracting them, the result is an even, efficient coat.
3.wrongFeedback: Think about like charges: they REPEL. If all droplets carry the same charge, they push each other apart — this spreads them out evenly across the surface. The car body (earthed, opposite charge) then attracts them all.

commonMistakes: Writing that protons transfer during friction charging — only electrons can move. Protons are locked inside the atomic nucleus and cannot be displaced by rubbing.
Saying 'like charges attract' — this is the opposite of the rule. Like charges REPEL; unlike (opposite) charges ATTRACT.
Drawing electric field lines around a negative charge pointing outward — field lines always point inward toward a negative charge (the direction a positive test charge would move).
Describing earthing as 'removing the electricity' rather than explaining that charge drains through the conducting earth wire to the ground, neutralising the object.
Confusing 'charged' with 'has more protons' — charge comes from gaining or losing electrons, not from changing the number of protons.
examTips: When asked to explain charging by friction, always state: (1) what transfers (electrons), (2) which way they move (from ___ to ___), and (3) what charge each object ends up with. Three steps, three marks.

For electric field questions, remember: field lines show the direction of force on a POSITIVE test charge. Around positive = outward. Around negative = inward. Between plates = parallel lines from + to − plate.

Uses of static electricity questions often ask you to explain in terms of charge — describe the charge on each component and the force between them. Don't just describe what happens; explain why using the words 'attracted' or 'repelled'.
revisionCardBullets: Friction transfers electrons only — never protons
Object gains electrons → negatively charged; loses electrons → positively charged
Charge is conserved: charge gained = charge lost
Like charges repel; unlike charges attract
Force between charges decreases with distance
Electric field: region where a charge experiences a force
Field lines: direction of force on a positive test charge; positive source → outward; negative source → inward
Uniform field between parallel plates: parallel equally-spaced lines from + to − plate
Static danger: sparks can ignite flammable vapour — tankers and aircraft earthed before fuelling
Inkjet printer: charged droplets deflected by electric field to precise positions
Electrostatic precipitator: charged plates attract smoke particles in chimneys
Paint spraying: charged droplets repel each other (even spread); attracted to earthed car body
